results.possibleIdentity: Lee Jaffe (born 1950) is an American multidisciplinary artist, photographer, filmmaker, musician, and producer who was highly active in the experimental art scenes of the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in the Bronx and educated at Penn State University, Jaffe dropped out in 1969 to travel to Brazil, where he collaborated with key figures of the Tropicália movement, including Hélio Oiticica and Neville d'Almeida. Upon returning to New York in 1971, he became a central figure in the conceptual art and experimental film community centered around the alternative space 112 Greene Street. During this period, he was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) milieu and participated in the landmark 1971 MoMA exhibition 'Projects: Pier 18,' where he presented a work titled 'Parallel Fears.' His early film collaborations included 'Impact' with Vito Acconci and 'Brooklyn Bridge' with Gordon Matta-Clark. In 1972, Jaffe transitioned into the music world after meeting Bob Marley; he moved to Jamaica, became a member of the Wailers (playing harmonica), and produced seminal reggae albums such as Peter Tosh's 'Legalize It.' In the 1980s, he returned to visual arts, focusing on large-scale multi-media historical paintings and collaborating closely with Jean-Michel Basquiat. His work is held in major collections, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
